main_sections.title: Vocation
main_sections.poem_text:
(1)
When the gong sounds ten in the morning and
I walk to school by our lane,
Every day I meet the hawker crying,

There is nothing to hurry him on, there is no
road he must take, no place he must go to, no
time when he must come home.
I wish I were a hawker, spending my day in
the road, crying,

(2)
When at four in the afternoon I come
back from the school.
I can see through the gate of that
house the gardener digging the ground.
He does what he likes with his spade,
he soils his clothes with dust, nobody
takes him to task if he gets baked in the
sun or gets wet.
I wish I were a gardener digging away
at the garden with nobody to stop me
from digging.

(3)
Just as it gets dark in the evening and my
mother sends me to bed,
I can see through my open window the
watchman walking up and down.
The lane is dark and lonely, and the
street-lamp stands like a giant with one red eye
in its head.
The watchman swings his lantern and walks
with his shadow at his side, and never once goes
to bed in his life.
I wish I were a watchman walking the streets
all night, chasing the shadows with my lantern.
– Rabindranath Tagore
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Written by Rabindranath Tagore, this three-stanza poem captures a child's longing for freedom and the romantic appeal of working life outside school. The speaker, a schoolchild, observes three workers at three different times of day — a bangles hawker in the morning, a gardener in the afternoon, and a night watchman at dusk — and wishes to be each in turn. The central theme is the child's perception of freedom: each worker seems unbound by rules, timetables, and constraints, unlike the speaker who must attend school and go to bed on time. The tone is wistful and imaginative, with a refrain of 'I wish I were...' anchoring each stanza. Dominant imagery includes the gong marking school time, the spade and dusty clothes of the gardener, and the atmospheric street-lamp described as 'a giant with one red eye' — a vivid simile that heightens the night-time mystery. The poem gently subverts the adult view of work as burden, presenting it through a child's idealising eye as pure liberty.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
A. Answer the following questions.
1. Who is the speaker in the poem?
2. Why does the speaker want to be a hawker?
3. What does he like about the gardener?
4. What does the speaker compare the street lamp to?
5. On the basis of the poem that you have read, share the daily schedule of the speaker.

question_bank.stem: Who is the speaker in the poem?
Why does the speaker want to be a hawker?
What does he like about the gardener?
What does the speaker compare the street lamp to?
On the basis of the poem that you have read, share the daily schedule of the speaker.

oracy.section_name: Let us Think + Let us Listen + Let us Speak

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
B. Think and discuss.
1. Why does the speaker admire people with different vocations?
2. What are the things that you observe on your way to school and your way back home?

question_bank.stem: Why does the speaker admire people with different vocations?
What are the things that you observe on your way to school and your way back home?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us Listen: Sit in groups of five. Each group will discuss and create two questions on the poem Vocation using the question words from the WH-family. Note down the questions. Now, put your textbooks back in your bags. Each group will get to share their questions with the class. One group will present their questions, and the remaining groups will answer the questions. This will continue till all the groups have shared their questions.

Let us Speak B. Listen and speak: The speaker of the poem wants to do many things.
• Think of a time when you wanted to do many things but could not.
• What were the things you wanted to do?
• What difficulties did you face in doing many things at once?
• How did it make you feel?
• Share your views with your classmates.

question_bank.stem: Think of a time when you wanted to do many things but could not. What were the things you wanted to do?
What difficulties did you face in doing many things at once? How did it make you feel?

word_work.section_name: Let us Learn + Let us Speak

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
A. Here are some of the words from Vocation. Read the poem and match each word with its meaning.
Gong — A metal disc that produces a loud sound when hit with a small hammer
Spade — A tool used for gardening
Lane — A narrow path
Giant — An imaginary man-like creature of great height and strength
Lantern — A case that holds a candle or light in any form and can be easily carried

question_bank.stem: Match: Gong
options: A tool used for gardening
An imaginary man-like creature of great height and strength
A case that holds a candle or light in any form and can be easily carried
A metal disc that produces a loud sound when hit with a small hammer
A narrow path

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
B. There is one mistake in each sentence given below. Underline the mistakes and rewrite the correct sentences in the given space.
1. I has recently read a new book.
2. The speaker goes for school at ten in the morning.
3. The fox ran fast than the others.
4. Mita was slept when the doorbell rang.
5. The old elephant walked slow to the river.
6. Radhika is tall than Suman.
7. Sudhanshu ran faster of all the runners.
8. My mother are cooking.
9. He go to school everyday.
10. The birds is flying.

question_bank.stem: Find and correct the error: I has recently read a new book.
Find and correct the error: The speaker goes for school at ten in the morning.
Find and correct the error: The fox ran fast than the others.
Find and correct the error: Mita was slept when the doorbell rang.
Find and correct the error: The old elephant walked slow to the river.
Find and correct the error: Radhika is tall than Suman.
Find and correct the error: Sudhanshu ran faster of all the runners.
Find and correct the error: My mother are cooking.
Find and correct the error: He go to school everyday.
Find and correct the error: The birds is flying.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: C. Look at the pictures and read the words written under them: telescope, hammer, camera, screwdriver, sickle, stethoscope, grater, tongs, compass, mouse, saw. Have you seen these objects? Where have you seen them? What are they used for?

visual_stimulus.brief_description: Labelled pictures of eleven tools and instruments (telescope, hammer, camera, screwdriver, sickle, stethoscope, grater, tongs, compass, mouse, saw) for vocabulary discussion

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us Speak A. Listen and repeat: Your teacher will say aloud the following words. Listen carefully and repeat after the teacher.
cushion  ration
fashion  tension
mention  attention
nation   vocation

1. What's missing?
The child sees only the fun part of each job. What do you think might be the difficult or boring part of being a hawker, a gardener or a watchman? Write a paragraph to express your thoughts.

question_bank.stem: Write a paragraph about the difficult or boring part of being a hawker, a gardener, or a watchman.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
2. In the poem, the speaker tells us about the vocations of different people. Write the vocations of any three of your family members, in 50–60 words each, from the list below.
Mother, Grandmother, Aunt, Sister, Father, Brother, Uncle

question_bank.stem: Write about the vocation of family member 1 (50–60 words).
Write about the vocation of family member 2 (50–60 words).
Write about the vocation of family member 3 (50–60 words).

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
3. What vocations are the following people associated with?
• A.P.J. Abdul Kalam
• M. Visvesvaraya
• Janaki Ammal
• M.S. Subbulakshmi
• Salim Ali

question_bank.stem: What vocation is A.P.J. Abdul Kalam associated with?
What vocation is M. Visvesvaraya associated with?
What vocation is Janaki Ammal associated with?
What vocation is M.S. Subbulakshmi associated with?
What vocation is Salim Ali associated with?

beyond_text.section_name: Let us Do + Let us Explore

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us Do — Job Mix-Up! There is something unusual in the given pictures. Notice carefully the things that you find strange. For example: A chef's job is to cook delicious meals, not to drive a fire engine. That's something firefighters do. Write two sentences in your notebook for each picture, following the example provided for you.

visual_stimulus.brief_description: Six pictures each showing a professional doing another profession's job (e.g. chef driving a fire engine, doctor directing traffic, firefighter teaching in classroom, lawyer working on building plans, construction worker at shoe repair, postman examining a patient) — learners identify the mix-up

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us Explore: Look at the illustrations of vocations — beekeeper, photographer, teacher, dancer, chef, pilot, farmer, florist.
A. Which vocation from the above illustrations would you choose for yourself? And why?
B. Is there any other vocation not shown in the pictures that you would like to take up as your dream job? Support your answer with a reason.
C. Draw yourself in the selected vocation in the blank space provided.

question_bank.stem: Which vocation from the illustrations would you choose for yourself? And why?
Is there any other vocation not shown in the pictures that you would like to take up as your dream job? Support your answer with a reason.
Draw yourself in the selected vocation in the blank space provided.

visual_stimulus.brief_description: Illustrations of eight vocations: beekeeper, photographer, teacher, dancer, chef, pilot, farmer, florist — used as stimulus for choice and discussion
